system_prompt:
You are distilling one chapter of a guitar method book into a short summary.

The user will give you a chapter file with topic-organized verbatim quotes
plus the human-rationale notes for each. Write a one-paragraph summary (4-7
sentences) that captures:

  - what this chapter teaches (the techniques, concepts, rules, examples)
  - the master's characteristic moves visible in this chapter (if any)
  - the load-bearing terminology this chapter introduces

Reference the topic labels from the chapter file when useful. Do not invent
information not present in the chapter. If the chapter is biographical or
introductory rather than instructional, say so explicitly.

Output just the paragraph — no preamble, no JSON, no markdown formatting.
user_prompt:
Chapter 4: I iii IV V (I)

<<<CHAPTER FILE
# Chapter 4 — I iii IV V (I)

_Pages 15-16._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## missing chord tones rule

> Usually these missing tones are either the 5th or the root, as mentioned before, but don’t be shocked if any other
> tone is left out. The main thing to concern yourself with is learning good sounding chord forms, and learning how to
> use them, and these two points are what this book is about.

**Significance (p. 15).** States the governing principle for chord voicing throughout the method: omitting the 5th or root is normal, and ear-appeal over theoretical completeness is the priority.

## diatonic triads in C

> The diatonic triads in the key of C are: C Dm Em F G Am B®

**Significance (p. 15).** Establishes the foundational seven diatonic triads in C.

## diatonic 7th chords in C

> The diatonic 7th chords in the key of C are: C# Dm7 Em7 F?# G7 Am? Bm7b5

**Significance (p. 15).** Lists the seven diatonic 7th chords with OCR noise intact.

## extensions defined

> A complete list of the most commonly used diatonic chords in the key of C including the EXTENSIONS
> (extensions are chords that add other diatonic notes on to the diatonic triads or diatonic 7th chords) is: (capital
> Roman numerals indicate a major triad)

**Significance (p. 15).** Defines extensions and clarifies the Roman numeral case convention.

## I chord extensions

> I: C, CF, C6, C/9, C6/9, C2, C45, (C#/6, Csus, C/9sus)

**Significance (p. 15).** Enumerates I chord voicings.

## IV chord extensions

> IV: F, F?, F6, F/9, F6/9, F9, F43, F/9+11, F6/9+11, F7+11, F9+11, (F7/6, F/+11, F43+11)

**Significance (p. 15).** Lists IV chord extensions, longest in the chapter.

## harmonic formulas defined

> these are two examples of what we will call HARMONIC FORMULAS (meaning
> progressions, that because they are so well liked, are constantly used by musicians as a basis for improvisation, compo-
> sition, arranging and what have you).

**Significance (p. 15).** Defines harmonic formulas as the book's central organizing concept.

## I iii IV V formula introduced

> The first one we are going to work with is: [iii TV V (1). Only triad symbols will be used for diatonic harmonic
> formulas {in other words, even if a progression uses, say C/9 Em7 F/9 G7sus C?, in a general sense it is still 1 int TV V I,
>
> even though specifically it is 1/9 iti? [V/9 V7sus 17).

**Significance (p. 15).** Introduces the chapter formula and the rule that harmonic formulas use triad terms regardless of extensions.

## register/transposition rule

> some chords, especially some very modern voicings coming up later, sound pretty terrible in low registers {that is,
> on the lower frets) so let your ears be the guide in determining how low to take any progression

**Significance (p. 15).** Practical rule: ear judgment overrides mechanical transposition when voicings become muddy.

## Baroque vs modern chord usage

> in the Baroque period of music
> (roughly 1600-1750), mostly triads and to a lesser extent, 7th chords, suspensions and added 9ths were used. Example:
>
> J F .
> 5 I ia
> é
> x 1 Zz x
> In modern times, the 7ths and extensions are used very often, with the triads playing a lesser role.

**Significance (p. 15).** Stylistic context for choosing voicings.

CHAPTER FILE>>>
